content.text: Azure Portal Dashboards are customisable pinboards that aggregate tiles — live metrics charts, resource status summaries, cost graphs, and markdown text — into a single view. A dashboard is a first-class Azure resource (type Microsoft.Portal/dashboards) stored in a resource group, which means it supports tagging, RBAC, ARM deployment, and export as JSON.

attrs.alt: Azure Portal Dashboard showing tile types: metrics chart, resource tile, resource group tile, markdown tile, all-resources list, clock, and cost chart

content.text: What a Dashboard Is

A dashboard is a collection of rectangular tiles arranged on a grid. Each tile is independent — it queries its own data source and updates on its own schedule. Tiles are resizable and draggable in edit mode. You can have multiple dashboards per account and switch between them from the sidebar.
Private dashboards: visible only to your account (the default when you create one)
Shared dashboards: published to a subscription, visible to users with Reader access on the dashboard resource
Default dashboard: the 'My Dashboard' created automatically when you first visit the portal; can be replaced with any dashboard as your startup page

A dashboard is stored in a resource group just like a VM or storage account. Deleting the resource group deletes the dashboard. Always pin dashboards to a long-lived resource group (e.g., rg-shared-ops) rather than a project-specific group.
Tile Types
The portal gallery offers many tile types. Understanding each type helps you build dashboards that give actionable information at a glance.
Tile Type
Data Source
Best For
Metrics chart
Azure Monitor Metrics
CPU%, memory, request count, latency — any metric from a resource
Resource
ARM resource properties
Status, location, and type of a single resource with a link to its blade
Resource group
ARM resource list
Count and types of resources in a group; quick navigation
All resources
ARM resource list (filtered)
Filtered list of resources across subscriptions; sortable
Markdown
Static HTML you write
Team links, runbook notes, incident contacts, architecture diagrams
Clock
Browser time
UTC or local time reference for on-call teams
Cost chart (Cost Management)
Azure Cost Management
Accumulated spend vs budget, trend by day/week
Service Health
Azure Service Health
Live status of Azure services and regions your resources depend on
Iframe
Any public URL
Embed external status pages or BI reports inside the portal
Application Insights chart
App Insights telemetry
Request rate, failure rate, response time for apps monitored by App Insights
Creating and Editing a Dashboard
Creating a New Dashboard
In the portal, click 'Dashboard' in the left sidebar
Click '+ New dashboard' > 'Blank dashboard'
Enter a name in the top bar (the name is editable after creation)
The dashboard enters Edit mode automatically: the tile gallery opens on the right
Drag a tile from the gallery onto the canvas
Resize by dragging the tile's lower-right corner
Click 'Save' to exit edit mode
Entering Edit Mode on an Existing Dashboard

# Two ways to enter edit mode:
1. Click "Edit" in the command bar at the top of the dashboard
2. Press Alt+E while viewing a dashboard

# In edit mode:
- Drag tiles to reposition
- Drag the lower-right corner of any tile to resize
- Click the pencil (✎) icon on a tile to change its settings
- Click the X on a tile to remove it
- Click "Add tile" in the command bar to open the gallery
- Click "Save" to persist changes, "Discard" to cancel
Pinning Items from Other Blades
The fastest way to add tiles is to navigate to a resource or metric you care about and pin it directly from that blade.

# Pin a metrics chart:
1. Open a resource (e.g., a virtual machine)
2. Click "Metrics" in the resource's left menu
3. Configure the chart (metric, time range, aggregation)
4. Click the pin icon (📌) in the chart's top-right corner
5. Choose which dashboard to pin to (new or existing)

# Pin a resource overview:
1. Open any resource blade
2. Click the push-pin icon in the top-right of the overview section
3. The resource tile is added to the chosen dashboard

# Pin an entire resource group:
1. Open a resource group blade
2. Click the push-pin icon in the top-right corner
3. A tile showing all resources in that group is added
Dashboard JSON Structure
Every dashboard is a JSON document. Exporting and understanding this structure lets you create dashboards programmatically, version-control them in git, and deploy them via ARM or Bicep.
Exporting a Dashboard

# Export via portal:
1. Open the dashboard you want to export
2. Click the kebab menu (...) in the command bar
3. Click "Download"
4. A JSON file is downloaded to your browser's Downloads folder
Dashboard JSON Schema
Deploying Dashboards with ARM and Bicep

Because dashboards are Azure resources, you can deploy them via ARM templates or Bicep files. This enables GitOps workflows where dashboard definitions are committed to a repository and deployed in CI/CD pipelines.
ARM Template for a Dashboard
Deploy via CLI

# Deploy a dashboard from an ARM template
az deployment group create \
  --resource-group rg-shared-ops \
  --template-file dashboard.json \
  --parameters dashboardName=ops-dashboard-prod

# Deploy from a downloaded dashboard JSON (add ARM wrapper)
# 1. Download dashboard JSON from portal
# 2. Wrap it in an ARM template resource block
# 3. Deploy:
az deployment group create \
  --resource-group rg-shared-ops \
  --template-file dashboard-arm-template.json

# List all dashboards in a resource group
az resource list \
  --resource-group rg-shared-ops \
  --resource-type Microsoft.Portal/dashboards \
  -o table

# Delete a dashboard
az resource delete \
  --resource-group rg-shared-ops \
  --resource-type Microsoft.Portal/dashboards \
  --name ops-dashboard-prod
Sharing Dashboards
Sharing a dashboard makes it a shared Azure resource. You control who sees it via RBAC. The recipient must have Reader access on the dashboard resource itself.
Share via Portal

# Share a dashboard:
1. Open the dashboard
2. Click "Share" in the command bar
3. Select the subscription and resource group to publish to
4. Click "Publish"
5. The dashboard is now a resource in rg-shared-ops (or whichever RG you chose)
6. Assign RBAC:
   - Reader: can view the dashboard and its tiles
   - Contributor: can edit the dashboard (but not necessarily the resources shown)
7. Recipients find it under Dashboard > "Browse shared dashboards"
Assign RBAC on a Dashboard via CLI

# Get the dashboard resource ID
DASHBOARD_ID=$(az resource show \
  --resource-group rg-shared-ops \
  --resource-type Microsoft.Portal/dashboards \
  --name ops-dashboard-prod \
  --query id -o tsv)

# Grant a user Reader access to the dashboard
az role assignment create \
  --role Reader \
  --assignee jane.smith@contoso.com \
  --scope "$DASHBOARD_ID"

# Grant a group access
GROUP_ID=$(az ad group show --group "ops-team" --query id -o tsv)
az role assignment create \
  --role Reader \
  --assignee-object-id "$GROUP_ID" \
  --assignee-principal-type Group \
  --scope "$DASHBOARD_ID"
Cloning a Dashboard

'Clone' in the portal makes a copy of the current dashboard with '- Clone' appended to the name. The clone is private by default. Use cloning to create team-specific variants of a base operations dashboard without rebuilding tiles.

# Clone via portal:
1. Open the dashboard to clone
2. Click the kebab menu (...) > "Clone"
3. A copy named "<original> - Clone" is created and opened in Edit mode
4. Rename and customise, then Save

# Cloning via JSON (programmatic):
1. Download the dashboard JSON (... > Download)
2. Change the "name" property in the properties section
3. Deploy as a new ARM resource in the same or different resource group
Common Pitfalls
Problem
Cause
Fix
Tile shows 'No data'
Time range too narrow, metric not emitted, resource stopped
Expand time range; check resource is running; verify diagnostics settings
Pinned metric chart shows different data than in-blade chart
Dashboard tile cached a different time range at pin time
Edit the tile and reset the time range to match
Shared dashboard not visible to a colleague
RBAC not assigned on the dashboard resource
Run az role assignment create with the dashboard resource ID
Dashboard deleted unexpectedly
The resource group containing it was deleted
Pin dashboards to long-lived resource groups (e.g., rg-shared-ops)
Edit mode not available
You only have Reader role on the dashboard
Request Contributor role on the dashboard resource from an Owner
'Download' exports an empty JSON
Dashboard has no lenses (no tiles added yet)
Add at least one tile, Save, then download
Building an Operations Dashboard Step by Step

A practical operations dashboard for an Azure-hosted application typically combines VM health metrics, storage availability, cost tracking, a Service Health tile, and a Markdown tile with on-call contacts. Here is a repeatable build procedure.
Step 1: Create the Dashboard

# Deploy a base empty dashboard via CLI before adding tiles
DASHBOARD_JSON='{
  "lenses": {"0": {"order": 0, "parts": {}}},
  "metadata": {"model": {}}
}'

az resource create \
  --resource-group rg-shared-ops \
  --resource-type Microsoft.Portal/dashboards \
  --api-version 2020-09-01-preview \
  --name ops-dashboard-prod \
  --location eastus \
  --properties "$DASHBOARD_JSON" \
  --is-full-object false

echo "Dashboard created. Open in portal:"
echo "https://portal.azure.com/#@<tenant>/dashboard/arm/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/rg-shared-ops/providers/Microsoft.Portal/dashboards/ops-dashboard-prod"
Step 2: Pin Metrics Tiles from Each Resource

# For each application resource, pin key metrics:

# Virtual machine CPU + memory:
1. Navigate to vm-prod-01 > Metrics
2. Add metric: Percentage CPU, aggregation = Avg
3. Click "Add metric" > OS Disk Read Bytes/sec
4. Set time range to "Last 24 hours"
5. Pin to ops-dashboard-prod

# Storage account availability:
1. Navigate to stprod001 > Metrics
2. Add metric: Availability, aggregation = Avg
3. Add metric: UsedCapacity
4. Pin to ops-dashboard-prod

# App Service response time:
1. Navigate to app-prod > Metrics
2. Add metric: Response Time, aggregation = Avg
3. Add metric: Requests
4. Pin to ops-dashboard-prod
Step 3: Add Cost and Service Health Tiles

# Cost tile:
1. Open "Cost Management + Billing" > Cost analysis
2. Configure view: Accumulated cost, current month, group by Service
3. Pin chart to ops-dashboard-prod

# Service Health tile:
1. Search "Service Health" in the portal
2. Click "Service issues" in the left menu
3. Configure the view to show only the regions your resources are in
4. Pin the health map to ops-dashboard-prod

# Budget status tile:
1. Go to Cost Management > Budgets
2. Open your monthly budget
3. Pin the budget donut chart to ops-dashboard-prod
Step 4: Add a Markdown Tile with Team Info

# Add a Markdown tile:
1. Click "Edit" on the dashboard
2. In the tile gallery, scroll to "Markdown"
3. Drag it onto the canvas
4. Click the tile's pencil (edit) icon
5. Paste Markdown content:

## On-Call Contacts
- Primary: alice@contoso.com | +1-555-0100
- Secondary: bob@contoso.com | +1-555-0101

## Quick Links
- [Runbook — VM restart](https://wiki.contoso.com/vm-restart)
- [Incident management](https://incidents.contoso.com)
- [CI/CD pipeline](https://devops.azure.com/contoso/my-project/_build)

## Deployment Status
Last deploy: **2024-06-30** by alice@contoso.com
Version: **v2.4.1** | [Release notes](#)

6. Click "Done editing"
7. Click "Save"
Automating Dashboard Management with PowerShell

Azure PowerShell provides the Az.Portal module and direct ARM API calls to manage dashboards programmatically. This is useful in CI/CD pipelines that need to update dashboard content when infrastructure changes.

# Connect to Azure
Connect-AzAccount
Set-AzContext -SubscriptionId "sub-production"

# List all dashboards in a resource group
Get-AzResource `
  -ResourceGroupName "rg-shared-ops" `
  -ResourceType "Microsoft.Portal/dashboards" | `
  Select-Object Name, Location, ResourceId

# Get dashboard JSON
$dashboard = Get-AzResource `
  -ResourceGroupName "rg-shared-ops" `
  -ResourceType "Microsoft.Portal/dashboards" `
  -Name "ops-dashboard-prod" `
  -ExpandProperties

# Export dashboard to file
$dashboard.Properties | ConvertTo-Json -Depth 20 | `
  Out-File -FilePath "ops-dashboard-prod.json" -Encoding utf8

# Update a dashboard from file
$updatedProps = Get-Content "ops-dashboard-prod.json" | ConvertFrom-Json
Set-AzResource `
  -ResourceGroupName "rg-shared-ops" `
  -ResourceType "Microsoft.Portal/dashboards" `
  -Name "ops-dashboard-prod" `
  -Properties $updatedProps `
  -ApiVersion "2020-09-01-preview" `
  -Force

Write-Output "Dashboard updated successfully"
Dashboard Access Control Patterns
Different teams need different levels of dashboard access. RBAC on the dashboard resource controls this without affecting access to the underlying Azure resources shown in the tiles.
Role
RBAC Role on Dashboard Resource
Can Do
Executive viewer
Reader
View the dashboard and all its tiles (read-only)
Team operator
Contributor
Edit tiles, resize, add/remove tiles from the dashboard
Dashboard owner
Owner
Edit the dashboard, share it, delete it, manage RBAC
No access
(none)
Cannot see the shared dashboard in the portal at all

Tile RBAC is separate from dashboard RBAC. A user with Reader on the dashboard but no Reader on the underlying subscription will see 'Access denied' in tiles that try to query that subscription's resources. Grant the minimum needed RBAC on each resource separately.

# Grant a team Reader access to the dashboard
DASHBOARD_ID=$(az resource show \
  --resource-group rg-shared-ops \
  --resource-type Microsoft.Portal/dashboards \
  --name ops-dashboard-prod \
  --query id -o tsv)

# Add all members of an AAD group
GROUP_OBJECT_ID=$(az ad group show --group "sre-team" --query id -o tsv)

az role assignment create \
  --role "Reader" \
  --assignee-object-id "$GROUP_OBJECT_ID" \
  --assignee-principal-type Group \
  --scope "$DASHBOARD_ID"

# Verify assignments
az role assignment list --scope "$DASHBOARD_ID" -o table
Dashboard for Cost Centre Monitoring
A cost-focused dashboard surfaces spending trends, budget status, and top cost drivers for finance stakeholders who do not need deep resource visibility.
Summary

Azure Portal Dashboards are RBAC-controlled Azure resources (Microsoft.Portal/dashboards) that aggregate tiles from metrics, resources, costs, and custom markdown into a shared or private view. Create dashboards by pinning elements from resource blades or dragging tiles from the gallery. Export dashboards as JSON for version control and redeploy them via ARM templates or the CLI. Share by publishing to a resource group and assigning Reader/Contributor RBAC. For large teams, maintain a GitOps repository of dashboard ARM templates deployed alongside infrastructure so operational views stay consistent across environments.
